environment_blocked_lines.text: The three-run primitive measurements cannot establish steady-state throughput, tail latency, energy efficiency, or portability.  The frozen-contract matrix provides warm-cache plaintext, gocryptfs, dm-crypt, AEGIS-Q, plus an fscrypt environment-blocked row; it is not yet a complete fscrypt, fs-verity, dm-integrity, OP-TEE, SPDK, or GPUDirect Storage comparison.  Its role is to expose the cost of the current authenticated-publication boundary, not to rank deployed encryption systems.  Historical fscrypt and dm-crypt fio outputs are retained only for traceability, not current comparison evidence, because they do not sweep the same request sizes, cache states, queue depths, sync modes, and application workloads.

Figure~\ref{fig:evaluation_summary} is the evaluation spine.  It deliberately puts the negative cost boundary next to the positive runtime effects: AEGIS-Q is slow under strict per-write authenticated publication, recovers bounded SQLite pressure relative to unthrottled storage while giving up background bandwidth, and uses the GPU only for a mounted maintenance workflow with explicit slack.  Table~\ref{tab:benchmark_workloads} states what each experiment can and cannot establish.  The frozen filesystem contract fixes 4~KiB 70/30 random read/write, \texttt{psync}, per-write \texttt{fdatasync}, queue depth~1, one client, a 1~GiB file, fixed mounts/platform, and five repetitions.  A same-profile attribution probe on the current code-owned binary confirms that the strict path still pays data-sidecar, journal-sidecar, and marker/checkpoint durability boundaries on each write-publication cycle.  The matched dm-crypt row now runs on the same warm-cache contract, passing five repetitions with median throughput 9.03~MiB/s and median p99/p99.9 latency 264/823~\(\mu\)s; fscrypt remains environment-blocked because \texttt{CONFIG\_FS\_ENCRYPTION} is unset and \texttt{fscrypt status} reports the root ext4 encrypt feature unavailable.
RQ2 Frozen contract & Plain/gocryptfs/dm-crypt/AEGIS-Q warm; fscrypt unavailable by env. proof & fscrypt throughput and cold-cache rows remain unavailable\\

frozen_contract_lines.text: The three-run primitive measurements cannot establish steady-state throughput, tail latency, energy efficiency, or portability.  The frozen-contract matrix provides warm-cache plaintext, gocryptfs, dm-crypt, AEGIS-Q, plus an fscrypt environment-blocked row; it is not yet a complete fscrypt, fs-verity, dm-integrity, OP-TEE, SPDK, or GPUDirect Storage comparison.  Its role is to expose the cost of the current authenticated-publication boundary, not to rank deployed encryption systems.  Historical fscrypt and dm-crypt fio outputs are retained only for traceability, not current comparison evidence, because they do not sweep the same request sizes, cache states, queue depths, sync modes, and application workloads.

Figure~\ref{fig:first_page_qos} is the paper's edge-runtime result.  Unthrottled secure-storage pressure reports 9.62~ms SQLite p99 and 6.98~MB/s background writes; AEGIS-Q reports 8.15~ms p99 and 3.02~MB/s by throttling only elastic writes.  The point is visible storage-layer policy with accelerator-independent format correctness, not peak filesystem throughput.  The low frozen-contract throughput row is a known cost boundary, not a hidden general-purpose claim.

\item \textbf{RQ2: Mode-aligned baselines.} What cost and bottleneck boundary does the FUSE prototype impose when the same authenticated publication rule is compared with plaintext and gocryptfs rows under the frozen filesystem contract?

\caption{Evaluation summary generated from retained validation JSON.  Panel~(a) shows the strict frozen-contract cost boundary for RQ2; Panel~(b) shows mounted SQLite QoS and kernel-control rows for RQ3; Panel~(c) shows the mounted open-file key-plane refresh workflow for RQ5.}

Figure~\ref{fig:evaluation_summary} is the evaluation spine.  It deliberately puts the negative cost boundary next to the positive runtime effects: AEGIS-Q is slow under strict per-write authenticated publication, recovers bounded SQLite pressure relative to unthrottled storage while giving up background bandwidth, and uses the GPU only for a mounted maintenance workflow with explicit slack.  Table~\ref{tab:benchmark_workloads} states what each experiment can and cannot establish.  The frozen filesystem contract fixes 4~KiB 70/30 random read/write, \texttt{psync}, per-write \texttt{fdatasync}, queue depth~1, one client, a 1~GiB file, fixed mounts/platform, and five repetitions.  A same-profile attribution probe on the current code-owned binary confirms that the strict path still pays data-sidecar, journal-sidecar, and marker/checkpoint durability boundaries on each write-publication cycle.  The matched dm-crypt row now runs on the same warm-cache contract, passing five repetitions with median throughput 9.03~MiB/s and median p99/p99.9 latency 264/823~\(\mu\)s; fscrypt remains environment-blocked because \texttt{CONFIG\_FS\_ENCRYPTION} is unset and \texttt{fscrypt status} reports the root ext4 encrypt feature unavailable.
RQ2 Frozen contract & Plain/gocryptfs/dm-crypt/AEGIS-Q warm; fscrypt unavailable by env. proof & fscrypt throughput and cold-cache rows remain unavailable\\

cold_cache.reason: cold-cache contract requires privileged cache dropping after unmount/remount; this run had no noninteractive sudo/root access
